sentences.text: The other thing is I would like to go into my childhood.
When you ask the questions [INAUDIBLE]
Edith Hass.
My name is Edith Hass-- maiden name is Neiderman.
It's November 3, 1926.
I'm 69 years old-- I'll be 69.
In Satu Mare, Romania.
My father's name was Abraham Neiderman.
My mother's maiden name was Szerena [?
Chassa. ?]
Yes, I had three brothers and two sisters.
The oldest was Goldie, Lillie.
My brother was Vernath, Daniel, and Hercu.
I grew up in Satu Mare.
I went to school there until 1944.
It was a town of about 55,000 people, and 14,000 were Jewish people living there.
We had a two-family house.
We shared it with my uncle.
And we were comfortable.
My father had a restaurant, and we had a very peaceful and loving family relation with my aunts and uncles.
We had a very, very nice family life.
There were cooked dishes and cold cuts and beverages, drinks, alcohol.
And, um, it was a nice comfortable living.
We made a nice-- my father made a nice living.
We were comfortable.
My home was a three-bedroom apartment.
And we lived with my grandfather, who I was very close to.
And, um--
His name was Lepod.
And I was very close with him.
He was retired already.
He was an older man.
He founded the business, and then he gave it over to my father and my gr-- my uncle.
Yes, we had about 32 uncles, aunts and uncles, and cousins, about 70, I guess, more or less. .
In the same-- some of them lived in the same town.
Some of them lived in the Hungary, and some of them lived in Czechoslovakia.
The one who lived out of the country, no.
Occasionally, my aunts came to visit my grandfather.
We spent some holidays together, and then we visited occasionally, but not very often.
Oh, it was beautiful.
I felt very privileged and very rich.
And I appreciated, then, as a young child, the family life we had.
It was very close knit.
And even with the aunts and the cousins, we were very, very close.
Everyone, each of us, had the same age as a cousin, so we really needed no other friends.
We were always together.
With holidays in my house-- and they used to come to us because my grandfather-- the aunts and uncles.
The center was in our house for getting together.
Homemade, [LAUGHS] it was good.
No, I never did.
I never did.
I went to public school, elementary school.
And then I went to a lyceum, which was like high school, and-- but I didn't finish.
I only went for two years.
No, there was all kind.
No, not only Jewish.
It was not a private school.
It wasn't a parochial school.
My friends-- I had two schoolmates and then the cousins, which I was already close with.
For fun, we went to a movie or a theater, and we used to walk a lot.
We were Shabbat observers, so we walked.
There was a nice center of the town.
We got together, and we spent-- we had our fun.
They had American movies.
Shirley Temple was my big fan-- I was big fan of hers.
We'd see all those older movies, and there were, uh, nice movies to be seen then.
It was not restricted, or you were able to go to see anything because it was different time than this today.
My classes, as far as large classes, they were, I think, 30, 35.
We studied in Romanian language.
The second language was French, which I studied in high school.
But I still remember Romanian.
My mother tongue is Hungarian, because my parents, when they were born, and where they went to school was Hungary.
And in the house, we spoke Hungarian.
But when I started school, I learned the language, Romanian, which I was fluent in, but not practicing it today.
It's really hard for me to converse in Romanian, but I understand.
It was very, very nice.
There was ultra-orthodox people, which was the Satmar [INAUDIBLE], which was a very famous Rabbi Teitelbaum.
And there were other, smaller synagogues.
There was one synagogue my grandfather was the president of.
That was [?
Kevrich ?] [?
Mishnias, ?] which I don't even know the Hungarian name or the English name of it.
He was very respected.
He was a very intelligent and very smart man.
And they used to come to him for advice, and he was self-taught.
And he had lot of knowledge and very intelligent, and he was very respected in the community.
Education, it was-- they didn't emphasize for the boys, especially, because the religion was very, very important.
We were very strict Shabbat observers, and they went to yeshivas.
And they learned on their own.
They knew how to read and write in Romanian, but they didn't go to higher education.
I had a private tutor, a lady who tutored how to read in Hebrew the prayers and the meaning of the religion that you studied, but I didn't go to any Hebrew school.
Summers, well-- we used to have help.
We used to have a maid.
But in 1940, when the Hungarian occupied the country where we lived, they were not allowed to hire any help.
I had to help my mother, because we still had a big household and shopping and cleaning, which was a lot of work to be done.
So that's how I spent my time, which I wasn't happy about it then.
I was pretty-- sometimes complain, but I had no choice.
I had to do it.
Yes, yes.
We-- we Always had this-- a lot of things in common.
And, uh--
We were just very close.
Yes.
Yes, there was.
That started when the Hungarian occupied the city.
There was-- we had to wear the yellow stars.
Before that, also we felt it.
Little by little, they took away the right-- the license-- to sell certain things in stores.
Like alcohol beverages my father was not allowed to sell in the store.
That must have been in 1942. 1942.
1930S, under the Romanians?
No, there was here and there, but nothing extreme, nothing very uncomfortable.
Like they used to call you dirty Jew, here and there.
And, um-- or sometimes when they came home from the synagogue, the holiday, they used to throw rocks.
But it wasn't, uh, that many occasions that it happened.
The youngsters, the young, the students, the Romanians.
No, no, no, they didn't.
Then in 1944, the German occupied the city.
And, uh, then We knew already.
We heard stories which they did to other people like Poland and Slovakia, but we didn't know the details, how bad was.
They just-- we knew, that it's not going to be good.
We saw that black cloud is over us and something very bad is going to be.
But we didn't realize it.
We couldn't imagine the things that really happened later.
Yellow stars-- that had started by the Hungarians.
And, uh, then they went on the street.
They made you different remarks.
It's a pity that you're Jewish and, you know, what's going to be with you.
And they took the men to forced labor, which they took them to the border of Russia.
Ukraine was occupied already by the Germans?
The-- my brother-in-law went, and my cousins went, and we never saw them again.
That was in '43.
Well, I didn't go to school anymore then.
Uh, in '4-- by the time-- when the Hungarians came in, already I didn't go to school.
They forced you to, uh, bring, uh-- bring your school books and to write and to not observe your Shabbat.
And my father was very much against it, and he didn't want me to continue.
And that's how I--
Well, the boys still went to yeshiva.
And elementary schools-- the younger ones still went.
The others finished elementary school by that time.
And you felt the anti-Semitism, the remarks, what they-- and it was very hard to make a living because you weren't allowed to sell certain things that was rationed, like bread or sugar or flour.
So you just had the minimum, really, that you are able to survive on.
The restaurant closed up.
Before the ghetto.
It didn't-- it fell into the ghetto, and no businesses were conducted anymore in that time, really.
Our house fell into the ghetto, and two other families moved in with us.
It must have been in March, March or April.
Yes, it was very crowded, and we were very concerned and very, um, very worried what's going to be.
We knew that it's not going to be good.
Day by day, it was getting worse.
We didn't stay in the ghetto long, about two or three weeks.
The temple we did, but in the ghetto, we didn't because we couldn't.
There was no shul in the ghetto.
It was, uh-- they conducted the services.
It was-- everybody was worried and everybody was-- you know what the future is going to be, everybody is concerned.
But, uh, there was nothing happening in the synagogues then.
Yes, it was a small.
My father was in a small temple.
And um, it was, uh-- mostly, I knew everybody, and I had friends there.
And I was, in fact, very fortunate that I should be able to go and be there and listen to the prayers, and I enjoyed it.
And sitting next to my mother was a treat.
And, uh, children didn't stay, in those days, long in temple.
They came to a certain prayer, and they went home.
Like holiday clothes.
The rabbi-- there was no special rabbi.
It was a very small shul, and the people they conducted the services between themselves.
Well, when the ghetto came, it was very sad because you were very limited.
We were very crowded, and the food was scarce.
And, uh.
We were just very worried.
And, uh--
My sisters.
My sister came home with her children-- and the younger-- both sisters.
And she also-- the Younger one also had a child, and the older one had two children.
And uh, two other families with children, so it was really crowded.
The other families were just from the near town.
They came from the farms and from the smaller towns, and they concentrated them in the city.
So we knew them from before, and we got along.
There was no problem to--
--live with them.
Food-- there was rations.
There was-- every family got a certain amount.
We had yet form before.
My mother was able to bake her own bread yet because we had the flour, and we had-- but we still had enough to go by.

There were places, bakeries, that you had the card, the ration card, where you went and you picked up whatever that was coming to you, and also the sugar and also the whatever else was to be gotten, but very little.
I played with my nephews and my nieces.
I loved them.
And we went outside in the streets, and it was May-- beautiful.
The flowers were in bloom, and I was young, and I was-- that is so unfair what's happening.
I didn't have the life to live yet, and I don't know what tomorrow is going to be.
So when you're young, you don't take things so seriously, you know, as an older person.
But I was very concerned what's going to be, and most of the family should be together.
We were very afraid we were going to be separated.
And--
They heard a rumor that the youngsters will go to work and the old people and the little ones will stay home.
So my mother was worried, where will they go?
What will I do as a young girl?
What kind of work will they give us?
And what supervision is going to be?
But it didn't come to that, because after the two weeks, we left.
They deported us.
Yes.
Yeah, about.
There, uh-- there was on the gate, by the-- where the-- that was barricaded, the area, the outside.
But not in the ghetto, we didn't see that, no.
Well, uh, first of all, we had to go through a hold.
It was like a school.
And we had to give your jewelry and whatever you possessed-- your-- the women, their marriage bands.
I had earrings and a watch.
I had to give it to them.
In the ghetto.
There were there some soldiers and police.
They were Hungarians.
No, we didn't.
There was some Germans.
They occupied, but they didn't bear the Nazi uniform.
They just wore regular German uniforms.
Uh, then we went back home.
We-- of course we were sad that we had to give in what was-- you know.
But it didn't bother us so much, because we were more concerned what else?
What's-- what's going to be next?
What will they do to us next?
So they came, and they told us that we'll have to gather the most necessary belongings.
Some soldiers.
And we have to stay outside, line up front of the house and wait till the turn's going to be that we should-- we'll have to walk to the station.
This was in May.
The beginning of May '44, 1944.
The most necessary, like we had some food leftover, a change of clothing, and for the babies, the children's clothing and blankets.
And we had to carry it ourselves.
So there was a children's carriage, which we piled up on there whatever.
And we walked to the station with our neighbors whoever was-- and by this station, the cattle cars were waiting, and they crammed us in there.
Like, um, that they, um, take animals in, like cattles.
There was no chairs, no nothing, just the floor-- bare floor.
And we tried to squeeze in because it was very crowded.
They put us 80 people into one cattle car.
All the family, yes.
We were together-- my father, my mother and my brothers.
We all were together.
Then, we were traveling for three days-- three days and three nights--
--in that cattle car.
No, no, and there were small babies.
There was a mother with a newborn child.
And we had no water all this time.
And we was-- my mother used to go to the window was the thing, and beg for water for the babies.
But nobody listened, nobody.
They wouldn't give us.
It was three very long days, and, uh, it was very, very difficult.
Pardon me?
No, there's nobody to talk to.
And that's when the Hungarian gave us over to the Germans.
Then, they had German patrols.
And my mother spoke German.
And she begged that they should, you know, that nobody listened.
We didn't have any water, and it as very, very difficult.
Children was crying.
My sister fainted a few times.
The air was very stuffy, and it was a very, very hard ex-- very sad experience.
Yeah, we were praying.
My father was a very strong believer.
And then he said, what's going to be?
He says, don't worry, God will help us.
The help is going to come in.
And we questioned him.
We said Father, what now?
He says, what can I tell you?
We still have to hope.
We still have to hope.
My father had a beard, and we were afraid that they're going to pick on him.
So my sister had a scissor and cut his beard.
And he had the black beard, yet.
And then in the back and front of our eyes, he got white.
He became white.
He became an old man.
And we didn't know what's going to be.
We didn't know what's expecting us to happen.
We didn't realize.
We couldn't imagine.
We couldn't imagine.
While we were home, before the ghetto, my brother came home one day very said, and he put on his hat.
And we couldn't talk to him.
And I asked him what happened.
He says, I cannot tell you what I heard today.
Just please, tell us.

Somebody who ran away from Slovakia-- which they occupied, the Germans, before our town, and they took the people away from there-- and he told a horror story about what's happening to people that the Germans take away.
We didn't want to believe it.
He said this is just a rumor.
It can't be.
It's impossible that it should be true.
They told him that there is such place as Auschwitz, and they have an exterminator and how they kill people and how they torture people.
And we didn't want to believe it, that it's true, that such a thing exists.
After the three days, [BACKGROUND NOISE] we arrived in Auschwitz, very confused and very tired and very exhausted.
They rushed us off on the wagon.
We weren't able to take anything with us.
They said, just leave everything.
You're going to get it later.
We didn't know.
We didn't know.
We didn't know.
And somebody came over to us which had the striped uniform, that they wore in camp, a man.
And he put his lapel-- under his lapel, he had the Magen David-- you know, a Star of David.
And he said, trust me, I'm Jewish, and listen to me.
I want your sister to give the child to an older person, and I looked much younger than I was.
And I want you to straighten yourself up, make yourself taller and older than you are.
And you're going to come to somebody that's going to ask you how old you are.
Tell that you're older, and make yourself look older.
And we took his advice, I didn't know.
We thought he's Jewish, he wants the good.
He's on our side.
And that's what I did.
And my--
No, no.
He was from Poland, I think.
He spoke Jewish.
And um, They separated us.
The men lined up separate and the woman, the ladies--
They had an order-- somebody who spoke to us German-- in German-- soldiers, must've been SS, I guess.
And that's what happened.
My father with my brothers, they were on one side, and my mother and the rest of the ladies, girls, went on the other side.
And then they stayed like five in a row.
And we stood there for awhile, and then the-- they started moving.
The line started to move.
And my sister didn't know what to do.
Should she listen to my-- to that certain person to told her to do what-- to give away the child.
And she did, and the older sister said what shall I do?
She says, shall I give my children also to an older person?
And she was looking around to who she would give it to.
But then she decided no, I'm not.
Whatever happens to my children, I want to be with them.
I'm not going to separate myself.
How old were all the children?
There was 5 and 3.
Yes, her name was-- the little girl's name was Miriam, and the little boy was named David.
And my other sister's was Taube.
She had one child who was about four years ago.
Pardon me?
The day like-- it was-- it was-- I don't know the weather.
We were just very frustrated, and I didn't even realize what the weather was like.
When we were on line-- so always five people went ahead to-- in the line.
And when it came to our line.
We stopped.
And there was Mengele.
You heard about him.
He was the one who selected people which way to go.
And he pointed to my mother and my sister, who was ever in that line-- I had the cousin also who was younger than me-- to the left.
Later, I know.
I didn't know then.
I didn't, but I saw picture, and then I know.
And he pointed to the left to my mother and my sister, and to me, I was myself, then my-- to the right.
And I said, I want to go with my mother.
I don't want to-- that's my mother.
I spoke a little German.
He says, no, you go.
And he had a rubber stick in his hand, pointed to my back and give me a push like I should go the other side.
And I stopped.
I went.
There was two doors, like there was a gate.
And I look back-- yes, you have to stop.
